en.extract:
The orange-breasted sunbird (Anthobaphes violacea) is a species of small, predominantly nectar-feeding bird that is endemic to the fynbos shrubland biome of southwestern South Africa. It is the only member of the genus Anthobaphes, in the family Nectariniidae (the sunbirds and spiderhunters), though it is sometimes placed in the genus Nectarinia. The birds are sexually dimorphic, with females being olive green while the males are orange to yellow on the underside with bright green, blue and purple on the head and neck.


Taxonomy
In 1760 the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson included a description of the orange-breasted sunbird in his Ornithologie, based on a specimen collected from the Cape of Good Hope. He used the French name Le petit grimpereau a longue queue du Cap de Bonne Espérance and the Latin Certhia Longicauda Minor Capitis Bonae Spei. Although Brisson coined Latin names, these do not conform to the binomial system and are not recognised by the International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature. When in 1766 the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus updated his Systema Naturae for the twelfth edition, he added 240 species that had been previously described by Brisson. One of these was the orange-breasted sunbird. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the binomial name Certhia violacea and cited Brisson's work. This species is now the only member of the genus Anthobaphes that was introduced by the German ornithologists Jean Cabanis in 1850. The name is from the Ancient Greek anthobaphēs "bright-coloured" derived from ανθος anthos for flower and βαφη baphē for dyeing.


Description
As with other sunbirds the bill is long and decurved, that of the male being longer than that of the female. The bill, legs and feet are black. The eyes  are dark brown. The head, throat and mantle of the male are bright metallic green. The rest of the upper parts are olive green. The upper breast is metallic violet and the lower breast is bright orange, fading to paler orange and yellow on the belly. The tail is long and blackish, with elongated central tail feathers, which extend beyond the other feathers. The female has olive-greenish grey upperparts and olive yellowish underparts, paler on the belly. The wings and tail are blackish. The juvenile resembles the female.
The birds' call is a twangy, weak ssharaynk or sskrang, often repeated several times.


Distribution and habitat
Due to its restricted range within the fynbos biome of South Africa's Western Cape, this sunbird is associated with ericas and proteas. It breeds when the heath flowers, typically in May. The male defends its territory aggressively, attacking and chasing intruders.
This tame species is a common breeder across its limited range, and is an altitudinal migrant, moving to higher altitudes during the southern summer in search of flowers. It is gregarious when not breeding, forming flocks of up to 100 birds.


Behaviour


Breeding
The orange-breasted sunbird breeds from February to November (mainly in May–August). The nest, built mainly by the female, is an oval of rootlets, fine leafy twigs and grass, bound together with spider webs and lined with brown protea fluff. It has a side top entrance, but does not have a covered porch. The usual clutch is two eggs and the female alone incubates. The eggs hatch in about 14.5 days and both parents feed the young. The young birds are mostly fed with insect and spider prey.


Food and feeding
The orange-breasted sunbird subsists on flower nectar, predominantly from ericas and proteas, although it will make use of other types of flowering plants as well. It will also take small insects and spiders, often in flight.


Ecology
Orange-breasted sunbirds are known to pollinate Protea, Leucospermum, and Erica species, the flowers of which they visit for nectar. They perch on the ground to visit the low flowers of Hyobanche sanguinea and Lachenalia luteola. They also indulge in nectar theft from flowers with longer corolla tubes such as Chasmanthe floribunda. Being fire-prone, the fynbos habitat ensures a great amount of mobility of the birds, which may have contributed to a greater level of individual genetic variability despite having a rather limited distribution range.
A number of plasmodia-like blood parasites are known from the orange-breasted sunbirds.


Conservation status
This species is currently classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. It may however be adversely affected by urbanisation, habitat conversion to agriculture, and fynbos fires.


References

The '''orange-breasted sunbird''' ('''''Anthobaphes violacea''''') is a species of small, predominantly nectar-feeding bird that is [[endemism|endemic]] to the [[fynbos]] shrubland [[biome]] of southwestern [[South Africa]]. It is the [[monotypic|only member]] of the [[genus]] '''''Anthobaphes''''', in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[sunbird|Nectariniidae]] (the [[sunbird]]s and [[spiderhunter]]s), though it is sometimes placed in the genus ''[[Nectarinia]]''. The birds are [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]], with females being olive green while the males are orange to yellow on the underside with bright green, blue and purple on the head and neck.

==Taxonomy==
In 1760 the French zoologist [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] included a description of the orange-breasted sunbird in his ''Ornithologie'', based on a specimen collected from the [[Cape of Good Hope]]. He used the French name ''Le petit grimpereau a longue queue du Cap de Bonne Espérance'' and the Latin ''Certhia Longicauda Minor Capitis Bonae Spei''.<ref name=brisson>{{ cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode contenant la division des oiseaux en ordres, sections, genres, especes & leurs variétés | volume=3 | language=fr, la | place=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche | pages=649–651, Plate 33 fig 6 | url= https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35953752 }} The two stars (**) at the start of the section indicates that Brisson based his description on the examination of a specimen.</ref> Although Brisson coined Latin names, these do not conform to the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial system]] and are not recognised by the [[International Commission on Zoological Nomenclature]].<ref name=allen>{{cite journal |last=Allen | first=J.A. | author-link=Joel Asaph Allen | year=1910 | title=Collation of Brisson's genera of birds with those of Linnaeus |journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History |volume=28 | pages=317–335 | hdl=2246/678 }}</ref> When in 1766 the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] updated his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' for the [[12th edition of Systema Naturae|twelfth edition]], he added 240 species that had been previously described by Brisson.<ref name=allen/> One of these was the orange-breasted sunbird. Linnaeus included a brief description, coined the [[binomial name]] ''Certhia violacea'' and cited Brisson's work.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1766 | title=Systema naturae: per regna tria natura, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=12th | volume=1, Part 1 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii  | place=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=la | page=188 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42946384 }}</ref> This species is now the only member of the genus ''Anthobaphes'' that was introduced by the German ornithologists [[Jean Cabanis]] in 1850.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49584482|title=Museum Heineanum: Verzeichniss der ornithologischen Sammlung des Oberamtmann Ferdinand Heine, auf Gut St. Burchard vor Halberstadt|last1=Cabanis|first1=Jean|last2=Heine|first2=Ferdinand|publisher=R. Frantz|year=1850|volume=1|place=Halberstadt|page=103|language=de, la|author1-link=Jean Cabanis|author2-link=Ferdinand Heine}}<!--IOC 8.1 gives year as 1850, H&M4 v2 p. 269 gives year as 1851--></ref> The name is from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''anthobaphēs'' "bright-coloured" derived from ανθος anthos for flower and βαφη baphē for dyeing.<ref name=hbwkey>{{cite web | last=Jobling | first=J.A. | year=2018 | title= Key to Scientific Names in Ornithology | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher=Lynx Edicions | url=https://www.hbw.com/dictionary/definition/anthobaphes | access-date=2 April 2018 }}</ref>

==Description==
As with other sunbirds the [[beak|bill]] is long and decurved, that of the male being longer than that of the female. The bill, legs and feet are black. The eyes  are dark brown. The head, throat and mantle of the male are bright metallic green. The rest of the upper parts are olive green. The upper breast is metallic violet and the lower breast is bright orange, fading to paler orange and yellow on the belly. The tail is long and blackish, with elongated central tail [[feather]]s, which extend beyond the other feathers. The female has olive-greenish grey upperparts and olive yellowish underparts, paler on the belly. The wings and tail are blackish. The juvenile resembles the female.<ref name=sasol>{{cite book|last1=Sinclair|first1=Ian|last2=Hockey|first2=Phil|last3=Tarboton|first3=Warwick|last4=Ryan|first4=Peter|title=Sasol Birds of Southern Africa: The Region's Most Comprehensively Illustrated Guide|publisher=Struik|year=2011}}</ref>

The birds' [[bird vocalization|call]] is a twangy, weak ''ssharaynk'' or ''sskrang'', often repeated several times.<ref name=sasol/>

==Distribution and habitat==
Due to its restricted range within the [[fynbos]] biome of South Africa's [[Western Cape]], this sunbird is associated with [[Erica (plant)|erica]]s and [[protea]]s. It breeds when the [[heath]] flowers, typically in May. The male defends its territory aggressively, attacking and chasing intruders.

This tame species is a common breeder across its limited range, and is an altitudinal [[bird migration|migrant]], moving to higher altitudes during the southern summer in search of flowers. It is gregarious when not breeding, forming flocks of up to 100 birds.<ref name=sasol/><ref name=sabap>{{cite web|url=http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/777.pdf|title=Orange-breasted sunbird - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds}}</ref>

==Behaviour==

===Breeding===
The orange-breasted sunbird breeds from February to November (mainly in May–August). The [[bird nest|nest]], built mainly by the female, is an oval of rootlets, fine leafy twigs and grass, bound together with [[spider]] webs and lined with brown protea fluff. It has a side top entrance, but does not have a covered porch.<ref name=sasol/><ref name=sabap/> The usual clutch is two eggs and the female alone incubates. The eggs hatch in about 14.5 days and both parents feed the young. The young birds are mostly fed with insect and spider prey.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Broekhuysen|first=G. J.|date=1963|title=The breeding biology of the orange-breasted sunbird Anthobaphes violacea (Linnaeus)|journal=Ostrich|language=en|volume=34|issue=4|pages=187–234|doi=10.1080/00306525.1963.9633478|bibcode=1963Ostri..34..187B |issn=0030-6525}}</ref>

<gallery widths="200px" heights="160px">
File:Female_Anthobaphes.jpg|Female collecting leaf hairs to line nest
File:Anthobaphes violacea 273464585.jpg|Nest placed in [[fynbos]] at [[Rooiels, South Africa|Rooiels]]
</gallery>

===Food and feeding===
The orange-breasted sunbird subsists on flower nectar, predominantly from ericas and proteas, although it will make use of other types of flowering plants as well. It will also take small insects and spiders, often in flight.<ref name=sabap/>

== Ecology ==
Orange-breasted sunbirds are known to pollinate ''Protea, Leucospermum,'' and ''Erica'' species'',''<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Coetzee|first1=A.|last2=Seymour|first2=C.L.|last3=Spottiswoode|first3=C.N.|date=2018|title=Investigating the origins of flower colour polymorphisms in sunbird-pollinated Erica (Ericaceae)|journal=South African Journal of Botany|volume=115|pages=282–283|doi=10.1016/j.sajb.2018.02.029|issn=0254-6299|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Johnson|first1=Christopher Michael|last2=He|first2=Tianhua|last3=Pauw|first3=Anton|date=2014-05-15|title=Floral divergence in closely related Leucospermum tottum (Proteaceae) varieties pollinated by birds and long-proboscid flies|journal=Evolutionary Ecology|language=en|volume=28|issue=5|pages=849–868|doi=10.1007/s10682-014-9712-0|bibcode=2014EvEco..28..849J |s2cid=1245380|issn=0269-7653}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Turner |first=R. C. |last2=Midgley |first2=J. J. |last3=Barnard |first3=P. |last4=Simmons |first4=R. E. |last5=Johnson |first5=S. D. |date=2012-03-01 |title=Experimental evidence for bird pollination and corolla damage by ants in the short-tubed flowers of Erica halicacaba (Ericaceae) |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0254629911001591 |journal=South African Journal of Botany |volume=79 |pages=25–31 |doi=10.1016/j.sajb.2011.11.003 |issn=0254-6299|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Turner |first=R. A. |date=2023-10-01 |title=Sympatric speciation in Erica mammosa (Ericaceae): Resprouters versus seeders and the roles of pollinators, moisture availability and fire |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0254629923004635 |journal=South African Journal of Botany |volume=161 |pages=333–346 |doi=10.1016/j.sajb.2023.08.012 |issn=0254-6299|doi-access=free }}</ref> the flowers of which they visit for nectar.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Zoeller|first1=K. C.|last2=Steenhuisen|first2=S.-L.|last3=Johnson|first3=S. D.|last4=Midgley|first4=J. J.|date=2016-03-15|title=New evidence for mammal pollination of Protea species (Proteaceae) based on remote-camera analysis|journal=Australian Journal of Botany|language=en|volume=64|issue=1|page=1|doi=10.1071/BT15111|issn=1444-9862}}</ref> They perch on the ground to visit the low flowers of ''Hyobanche sanguinea'' and ''Lachenalia luteola''.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Turner|first1=R.C.|last2=Midgley|first2=J.J.|date=2016|title=Sunbird-pollination in the geoflorous species Hyobanche sanguinea (Orobanchaceae) and Lachenalia luteola (Hyacinthaceae)|journal=South African Journal of Botany|volume=102|pages=186–189|doi=10.1016/j.sajb.2015.07.004|issn=0254-6299|doi-access=free}}</ref> They also indulge in [[Nectar robbing|nectar theft]] from flowers with longer corolla tubes such as ''Chasmanthe floribunda''.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Geerts|first=S.|date=2016-06-08|title=Can short-billed nectar thieving sunbirds replace long-billed sunbird pollinators in transformed landscapes?|journal=Plant Biology|language=en|volume=18|issue=6|pages=1048–1052|doi=10.1111/plb.12474|pmid=27219484|bibcode=2016PlBio..18.1048G |issn=1435-8603}}</ref> Being fire-prone, the fynbos habitat ensures a great amount of mobility of the birds, which may have contributed to a greater level of individual genetic variability despite having a rather limited distribution range.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Chan|first1=Chi-hang|last2=Vuuren|first2=Bettine Jansen van|last3=Cherry|first3=Michael I.|date=2011-04-01|title=Fynbos fires may contribute to the maintenance of high genetic diversity in orange-breasted sunbirds (Anthobaphes violacea): research article|journal=South African Journal of Wildlife Research|language=en|volume=41|issue=1|issn=2410-7220|hdl=10520/EJC117359}}</ref>

A number of plasmodia-like blood parasites are known from the orange-breasted sunbirds.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lauron|first1=Elvin J.|last2=Loiseau|first2=Claire|last3=Bowie|first3=Rauri C. K.|last4=Spicer|first4=Greg S.|last5=Smit|first5=Thomas B.|last6=Melo|first6=Martim|last7=Sehgal|first7=Ravinder N. M.|date=2014|title=Coevolutionary patterns and diversification of avian malaria parasites in African sunbirds (Family Nectariniidae)|url=http://userwww.sfsu.edu/sehgal/Publications_files/Lauron%20Africa%202014.pdf|journal=Parasitology|volume=142|issue=5|pages=635–647|doi=10.1017/s0031182014001681|pmid=25352083|issn=0031-1820|archive-date=2018-08-06|access-date=2018-08-06|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180806054936/http://userwww.sfsu.edu/sehgal/Publications_files/Lauron%20Africa%202014.pdf}}</ref>

==Conservation status==
This species is currently classified as [[least-concern species|Least Concern]] by the [[IUCN]]. It may however be adversely affected by urbanisation, habitat conversion to agriculture, and fynbos fires.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />
